# Gray Oak Journal

> Reflections on leadership, mentorship, and the practical challenges of guiding organizations and developing people. The ideas start in veterinary medicine, but they apply anywhere people lead people.

Author: Dr. Susan Ries Valashinas, DVM — veterinarian, hospital owner, mentor. [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/susan-valashinas-dvm-91784b195/)

Site: https://grayoakjournal.com

## Essays

- [When the Leader Is the Emergency: How to Lead Your Team Through Crisis and Trauma](https://grayoakjournal.com/when-the-leader-is-the-emergency/): What happens when the person your team relies on is the one who needs help. A real story about crisis leadership, debriefs, and what near-misses reveal about your culture.
- [The Tail on the Counter](https://grayoakjournal.com/the-tail-on-the-counter/): How strong veterinary leaders teach their teams to prioritize the right work, in the right order, at the right time. Sequence, deadlines, and why the reasoning matters.
- [She Did Not Need to Be Managed More. She Needed to Be Trusted More.](https://grayoakjournal.com/if-you-dont-trust-them-why-did-you-hire-them/): Why leaders fail to empower employees, and what it costs them. A practical look at hovering, micromanagement, and the bottleneck you built by accident.
- [The Family Crest Would Be a Donkey](https://grayoakjournal.com/the-family-crest-had-a-donkey-on-it/): Stubborn does not need instructions. A veterinarian on recognizing the trait in yourself, understanding why people resist change, and knowing when patience stops being leadership.
- [Lost in Translation](https://grayoakjournal.com/lost-in-translation/): If your client leaves the room confused, it does not matter how correct you were. Clear communication is not a soft skill. It is a clinical skill, a service skill, and a leadership responsibility.
- [The Lost Art of Porch Sitting](https://grayoakjournal.com/the-lost-art-of-porch-sitting/): Stillness is not the absence of leadership. It is the beginning of it. A veterinarian on awareness, observation, and the discipline of paying attention.
- [Some People Would Rather Quit Than Say Sorry](https://grayoakjournal.com/some-people-would-rather-quit-than-say-sorry/): When an employee walks out rather than apologize, it reveals a personality trait that can quietly destroy your veterinary team's culture and patient safety.
- ["Try Again Tomorrow": Because Today Was a Disaster](https://grayoakjournal.com/try-again-tomorrow/): Snapping doesn't make you a bad person. Refusing to reflect on it does. Self-reflection, apologies, and fixing the systems behind veterinary burnout.
- [Decision Fatigue Is Real. And So Is My Relationship With Reality TV](https://grayoakjournal.com/decision-fatigue/): When you make high-stakes decisions all day, your brain needs real rest. A veterinarian on decision fatigue, burnout, and building a daily off switch.
- [Rip the Band-Aid, Not the Skin](https://grayoakjournal.com/rip-the-bandaid/): How to fire someone without unnecessary damage to them, to you, or to your team. A step-by-step guide for veterinary leaders on handling terminations with clarity and dignity.
- [One Bad Apple Will Ruin the Whole Orchard](https://grayoakjournal.com/one-bad-apple/): Knowing when to fire someone in your veterinary hospital before the damage spreads. One bad apple really will ruin the bunch, especially when the bunch is already short-staffed.
- [The Unicorn in Sensible Shoes](https://grayoakjournal.com/the-unicorn-in-sensible-shoes/): We have become so accustomed to indifferent service that when someone simply does their job well, we talk about it as though we have spotted a unicorn in sensible shoes.
- [The Best Veterinarian in Town Can Still Lose to a Friendlier Front Desk](https://grayoakjournal.com/the-best-veterinarian/): Your hospital is not primarily a healthcare provider. It is a service business that happens to deliver healthcare. That distinction changes everything.
